description: Explains how marimo is installed, how the command-line interface introduces editing, running, tutorials, conversion, export, and how the main Click commands route those workflows.
document:
marimo's setup story is intentionally centered on the terminal. The installation guide recommends working inside a Python virtual environment before installing the package, so marimo and optional data-science packages do not collide with packages from other projects. Users can install the minimal package through pip, uv, or conda, then verify the install by launching the intro tutorial. The same guide separates the lightweight base install from the recommended extras: the base package keeps compatibility broad, while the recommended set unlocks editor features such as SQL cells, AI completion, dataframe-column plotting, formatting support, and notebook export to Jupyter format.

The quickstart presents the installed `marimo` command as the entry point to everyday work. Tutorials are the first validation path, editing creates or opens notebooks, running serves a notebook as an app with code hidden by default, conversion imports existing notebooks or scripts into marimo's Python file format, and export sends marimo notebooks to other formats. The quickstart also points out that a marimo notebook remains executable as a Python script, and that script-style CLI arguments can be passed through to notebooks for workflows that need parameters.

The root CLI is implemented in `marimo/_cli/cli.py` with Click. Its main group sets global behavior for logging, quiet output, automatic yes to prompts, and development mode, then registers subcommands from the surrounding CLI package. The help text is curated for first-time users rather than being only a mechanical command listing: it highlights the intro tutorial, editing a new notebook, running a notebook as an app, and asking the tutorial command for its own help. A compact usage-error hook and a colored help group are installed so command feedback follows marimo's terminal UX.

The edit path is the authoring server. `edit` accepts either a filename, a directory, no name, a URL after validation, or piped notebook contents on Unix-like systems. Existing files are checked before the server starts, missing notebook files are created as empty files, and no-name invocations open the current working directory. The command resolves sandbox mode after path validation, can delegate untrusted remote notebooks to Docker, checks shared-memory availability because edit mode communicates with kernel subprocesses, optionally performs an update check, and starts the server in edit session mode with code included and console output redirected to the browser.

The run path serves notebooks as read-only apps. It separates notebook paths from notebook arguments at a bare argument delimiter, validates files and directories, and can collect multiple files or directories into a gallery-style router. A single notebook may be checked before serving, while multi-notebook runs require the dependency used for separate kernels and cannot use the explicit check option. Sandbox handling differs between a single notebook and a gallery: single-file sandboxing can re-exec through uv, while galleries use per-notebook sandboxed kernels. The server starts in run session mode, excludes code unless requested, and uses a shorter default session lifetime after websocket disconnects.

Conversion and export are related but separate CLI surfaces. The convert command accepts Jupyter notebooks, Markdown or Quarto documents with Python fences, and Python scripts; it strips Jupyter outputs, extracts eligible Markdown code, leaves already-valid marimo notebooks unchanged, and may ask for the package needed to convert py:percent scripts. Export commands live under `marimo export` and share a helper that writes to an output file or stdout, prompts before overwriting unless forced, and supports watch mode only when an output file is provided. HTML and output-including Jupyter exports run the app before exporting so notebook arguments can affect results, while script and Markdown exports are static transformations.
